codings.submission_text:
Understanding Intersectionality 

 Intersectionality is a theoretical framework that examines how various social categorizations such as race, class, and gender interact on multiple levels to create overlapping systems of discrimination or disadvantage. 

 Key Aspects 

 The concept was first coined by **Kimberlé Crenshaw** in 1989 to address the marginalization of Black women within both feminist and anti-racist discourse. It recognizes that individuals possess multiple, layered identities that shape their lived experiences in profound ways. 

 Why It Matters 

 Furthermore, intersectionality provides a **critical lens** through which we can analyze **systemic inequality**. It demonstrates that social categories are not independent but rather interconnected, creating **complex systems** of privilege and oppression that affect individuals differently based on their unique combination of identities.

codings.submission_text:
Reading Crenshaw changed how I think about something that's been bothering me for years. My mom is from Tamil Nadu and she's also a woman and she's also an immigrant who didn't go to college here. I used to think about those things as separate facts about her. But the reading made me see they're not separate at all — they compound each other in a specific way.

Like when she goes to parent-teacher conferences, teachers talk slower to her even though she has a master's degree from Chennai. That's not just racism and it's not just sexism and it's not just some bias against her accent marking her as less educated — it's all three working together in that one moment where a teacher decides she doesn't need to be taken seriously. Intersectionality names that thing I knew was happening but didn't have language for.

What the framework gets right is that you can't fix one piece and call it done. Even if you fix the sexism you still have the accent bias. You need to look at the whole picture of how someone is being read in a room. That's what I took from this reading.

I also think the Crenshaw piece was written primarily about Black women in America and I wonder whether the framework travels to my mom's situation exactly the same way or if there's something slightly different about South Asian immigrant women. I don't have an answer but it's a question I keep coming back to.

codings.submission_text:
In my family we have a saying my mother taught me that translates roughly to 'a woman's burdens have many handles' — meaning the things that can grab you and pull you down come in many forms. I didn't know this had an academic name until this class.

My grandmother came from Guinea to France in the 1970s. She was Black, she was Muslim, she was poor, she was a woman, she was an immigrant, and her French was accented. In France she was too African. When she visited Guinea she was too French. She fit nowhere cleanly and was penalized for that in every space she entered. Crenshaw's framework gives me language for what my grandmother lived — not one discrimination piled on another like separate weights, but a kind of specific visibility that means you are always readable as 'not quite right' no matter where you stand.

I do think the framework is incomplete in one way. It was built on American categories and my grandmother's experience doesn't map cleanly onto American racial hierarchies. There's a whole layer about colonialism and francophone identity that intersectionality doesn't fully account for. But it's a better starting point than pretending identity is simple, and at least it gives you a way to talk about why the simple picture keeps failing people.

I've been thinking about how intersectionality shows up in who gets believed.

My cousin Valentina works in a hospital as a CNA. She's Mexican American, she's 22, she didn't finish college, and she's on the heavier side. When she tells a doctor that a patient is in pain or that something looks wrong, she gets ignored more than the older white nurses. She can't point to just one reason. Is it because she's Latina? Because she doesn't have a degree? Because of her age? Because of her size? It's all of it arriving at once and someone decides in a few seconds whether to take her seriously.

What the reading helped me see is that those seconds matter clinically. A patient doesn't get pain medication. A condition gets missed. The discrimination against Valentina isn't just unfair to her — it has actual downstream consequences on the people she's trying to help.

Intersectionality isn't just a framework for understanding oppression, it's a framework for understanding how things go wrong in systems that were built with a specific kind of person in mind as the default. The hospital wasn't designed with Valentina in mind and the reading gives me a way to say why that matters.

I wasn't expecting to care about this reading as much as I did. I've seen the word 'intersectionality' used online a lot, sometimes without much behind it. But reading Crenshaw's actual argument — the DeGraffenreid case, where Black women couldn't sue for race discrimination because Black men weren't affected, and couldn't sue for sex discrimination because white women weren't affected — stopped me cold.

Those women lost. Not because the discrimination didn't happen. Because the legal system was built to see race or gender, not both at once. The system literally could not see them. That's not an accident or an oversight, that's a structural choice about who counts.

I kept connecting it to things I see in my own school. The students who get suspended most aren't just Black students — it's specifically Black girls who get coded as 'aggressive' or 'threatening' in ways that white girls doing the same behavior never are. That's not just racism and not just sexism. It's something that only happens at that specific intersection and you can't fix it without naming it as such.

The framework is empowering in that way. Naming what's actually happening is the first step to changing it. The law couldn't see those women. I want to learn how to see people more completely than the systems that were built to process them.

I think the example from the reading about Black women in workplace discrimination cases was really clear. Like, you can't sue for race discrimination because there are Black men who weren't discriminated against, and you can't sue for sex discrimination because there are white women who weren't discriminated against. Personally I connect to this with my own family but I'm still figuring out how exactly. I might write more about that once I've thought it through. For now I just wanted to show that I understood the main argument and I think it's right.

Being Black and a man and working class aren't three different things you experience one at a time — they interact and create a specific experience that's different from someone who shares only one of those traits.

The reading made a good point about how a lot of civil rights frameworks focus on one axis at a time and that leaves people out. The example with Black women not being able to use existing legal protections because the protections were designed for either Black people or women was useful for understanding why a more complex analysis is necessary.

I can see how this applies to other groups — disabled LGBTQ people, Indigenous women, low-income immigrants. Each of those is a combination that creates something specific that a single-issue framework misses.

I think the reading was useful and the concept makes sense. I'm not sure I have much to add to it beyond agreeing with the argument and thinking it should be applied more widely in how we design laws and institutions.

codings.submission_text:
The reading on intersectionality highlights the complexities of identity and how different aspects of it interact to create unique experiences of privilege and oppression. The author argues that simply being a woman of color, for example, does not automatically mean that one experiences oppression. Rather, it is the intersection of factors such as race, class, and sexuality that determines one's positionality. This idea is particularly relevant in understanding the experiences of marginalized communities, as it emphasizes the need to consider multiple axes of identity.

I found it striking that the author uses the example of the "triple jeopardy" faced by black women in the Civil Rights Movement. Despite being a key figure in the movement, black women like Sojourner Truth and Ida B. Wells faced both racism and sexism. This intersection of oppressions highlights the ways in which different forms of oppression can intersect and exacerbate one another.

What struck me most about this reading was the emphasis on the need for a more nuanced understanding of identity and power dynamics. Simply being a member of a marginalized group is not enough to guarantee solidarity or shared experiences. Rather, we need to consider the specific ways in which different individuals intersect with multiple forms of oppression.

codings.submission_text:
Intersectionality is a crucial concept in ethnic studies that helps us comprehend the complexities of discrimination. Developed by Kimberlé Crenshaw, intersectionality acknowledges that individuals have multiple identities that intersect and overlap, creating unique experiences of marginalization. For example, a woman of color may face both sexism and racism, which cannot be addressed separately. Intersectionality recognizes that these forms of oppression are not mutually exclusive, but rather interconnected, leading to a more nuanced understanding of the ways in which social inequalities intersect.

By considering multiple identities and experiences, intersectionality sheds light on the ways in which dominant groups maintain power and privilege. It highlights the need to address the systemic inequalities that result from the intersections of racism, sexism, homophobia, and other forms of oppression. Intersectionality encourages us to move beyond a simplistic, additive approach to understanding identity and instead, to consider the complex ways in which multiple forms of oppression are experienced simultaneously. By doing so, we can develop more effective strategies for promoting social justice and challenging the status quo. Ultimately, intersectionality offers a powerful framework for analyzing and addressing the complexities of oppression in our society.

Intersectionality is the framework that Kimberlé Crenshaw developed to describe how different aspects of identity — race, gender, class, sexuality, disability — overlap and create compounded experiences instead of separate ones.

I think this reading was relevant to what we've been studying about how systems get built. Systems get built with a certain kind of person in mind and everyone else has to navigate around that. Intersectionality explains why navigating is harder for some people than others — because they're dealing with multiple ways the system wasn't designed for them.

In my neighborhood I see this with how policing works. Black men get policed one way, Black women get policed another way, and Black trans women get policed in a way that's completely different and far more dangerous. That's intersectionality showing up in something concrete and current.

I think this framework should be taught earlier. The sooner you have language for these patterns, the sooner you can name them when you see them.

I kept thinking about my friend Deja while reading this. She's Black and also deaf and navigating high school in ways none of the rest of us really understand. The deaf community has its own culture and its own discrimination issues, and then racial stuff layers on top of that, and sometimes they conflict. Like there are spaces that are welcoming to deaf people that are mostly white, and spaces that are welcoming to Black people where her disability is invisible or treated as secondary.

Intersectionality helps me understand why it can be so exhausting to try to fit into any single community when you belong to multiple groups that don't always talk to each other. You keep getting offered partial belonging.

My abuela's name is Esperanza and she came to this country when she was nineteen. She's seventy-two now and she still cleans houses. Not because she didn't try to do something else — she tried. But when you don't speak English and you're a woman and you came here without papers and you're sending money home to Oaxaca every month, your choices get narrow fast.

She became invisible in a specific way. The doctors talk to my mom instead of her even now, like she's not in the room. At the market people act like she doesn't understand what they're saying even when she does. But at church she's respected because she's been there longer than almost anyone, and she knows things about people's families going back thirty years.

I've thought a lot about why some places see her one way and other places see her another way. It's not just that she's old, or just that she speaks Spanish first, or just that she cleaned houses her whole life. It's all of those things at the same time, and they all weigh different depending on where she's standing.

I don't know the academic word for this. But watching her my whole life I know what it feels like when someone gets seen as a whole person and when they get reduced to just one thing about them.

codings.submission_text:
My mom always tells this story about when she first came here from Honduras. She was 22, she spoke no English, she was pregnant with my sister. She got a job at a textile factory and her boss knew she was undocumented and used that to keep her from saying anything when the conditions were bad. She worked around chemicals that weren't supposed to be used without ventilation. She got headaches every day. She didn't complain because she couldn't afford to lose the job and she couldn't afford anyone asking too many questions.

When she tells the story now she says she felt like she didn't exist. Not invisible exactly — people could see her. But they could act like her wellbeing didn't count. She was there to produce and if she got sick that was her problem.

What made her situation so hard wasn't any one thing. It was that she was a woman and pregnant and an immigrant and undocumented and didn't speak the language — and each of those things meant the people who could have helped her felt like they didn't have to. Any one of those things she might have found a way around. All of them together meant there was no way out.

I think that's what the reading is trying to describe. My mom lived it before I had words for it.

codings.submission_text:
My little brother Malik has ADHD and he's also Black and he's nine. I've been watching what's happening to him at his school and I have a lot of feelings about it.

When Malik can't sit still or blurts something out, he gets sent to the office. When I've been there and seen white kids in his class do the same thing, they get redirected or given a warning. Malik gets a discipline referral. My mom has been fighting this for two years. She pulls his records, she goes to meetings, she asks questions. But she works overnight shifts at the hospital and she's doing this alone and she is exhausted.

So what's happening to Malik is about his ADHD, because schools don't always know how to teach kids whose brains work like his. And it's about being Black, because Black boys get read as a threat before anyone else. And it's about not having money for a private therapist or an advocate. And it's about my mom being a single woman without a partner to split this work with.

I didn't learn any of this from a reading. I learned it from watching my family. But I recognize it in the reading now that I've done it.

codings.submission_text:
For this reflection I want to bring in something we didn't read in class. There's a podcast called Maintenance Phase — it's about wellness culture and health misinformation — and they did an episode about the history of the BMI. The BMI was developed in the 1800s using only white European men as the data set. That's it. And now it's the primary tool doctors use to evaluate health for everybody.

What that episode made clear is that the tool itself encodes the assumption that the white European male body is the standard, and everyone else is a deviation from it. So when a fat Black woman goes to the doctor and gets told her BMI is too high, she's being evaluated by a tool that was never designed with her in mind — and may be actively misleading about her actual health — but the doctor treats it as neutral science.

That's intersectionality in action. Her experience at the doctor is not just about being fat, or just about being Black, or just about being a woman. The belief that the tool is neutral makes it harder to question any single piece of it.

I know this wasn't assigned reading but I think it connects directly to what Crenshaw is arguing. The BMI is a framework built for one person and extended to everyone. So was the law Crenshaw was critiquing.

Ok so I'm just gonna be real with this one because I feel like that's what this assignment is asking for.

The reading is talking about something I already knew but didn't have a name for. Like I been knowing that being Black and being a girl is its own specific thing. It's not Black plus girl. It's like a whole different channel that comes with its own static.

The way teachers talk to me versus how they talk to my Black guy friends versus how they talk to white girls — it's different. Not always in a way you can point to and say that's the thing. It's more like a texture. The expectations are different. The patience is different. The benefit of the doubt is different.

Crenshaw is talking about Black women in 1989 courts but honestly it hits the same in 2026 high school hallways. The frame is the same. Who gets seen, who gets believed, who has to work twice as hard to get half the credit.

I learned this from watching my mama navigate the world, not from a textbook. She never said the word intersectionality but she showed me what it meant every time she had to code switch in a meeting or smile through something she should've been able to push back on.

This framework finally gave me academic language for lived reality. That's actually kind of powerful.

intersectionality

ok so I'm going to try to write this and hopefully it makes sense. the reading is about how people have multiple identities and they don't just add together, they interact. Crenshaw was saying Black women in the lawsuits couldn't use race discrimination because Black men weren't affected and couldn't use sex discrimination because white women weren't affected so they fell through this gap in between.

that gap is the intersection I think

feedback.draft_text: Maria Ndiaye, I appreciate the personal story you shared about your grandmother's experiences in Senegal, highlighting how intersectionality plays out in everyday life. Your statement, 'Intersectionality for me it is not theory it is just what my family live through,' resonated deeply. It takes courage to share personal experiences, especially those related to marginalization, and I acknowledge the emotional labor that goes into doing so. One of your strengths is applying the concept of intersectionality to your family's experiences, making the theory more relatable and tangible. For further exploration, consider how the inclusion of diverse voices from outside America, as you suggested, could enhance our understanding of intersectionality in different contexts.

Jordan, your reflection on how Crenshaw's work on intersectionality made you think about your mom's experiences as a Korean woman really stood out to me. You shared a powerful example of how intersectionality plays out in your own family, highlighting the unique experiences that arise from the overlap of race and gender. I appreciate the emotional labor you put into sharing this personal connection, and I'm grateful for your willingness to explore these complex ideas. For next week, I'm curious: how do you think we can apply the concept of intersectionality to create more inclusive spaces in our own communities? Your thoughts on this are valuable, and I look forward to hearing them.

Alex, your analysis of intersectionality as a 'critical lens through which we can analyze systemic inequality' really stood out to me. This insight, drawing on Kimberlé Crenshaw's work, demonstrates a deep understanding of how social categories intersect to create complex systems of privilege and oppression. I appreciate how you've applied these concepts to highlight the importance of considering multiple, layered identities. For further exploration, I'm curious: how do you think intersectionality can be applied in real-world scenarios to address these systemic inequalities?

Priya, your reflection on intersectionality, particularly how it 'names that thing I knew was happening but didn't have language for,' really stood out to me. It takes a lot of courage to share personal experiences, like your mother's as a Tamil Nadu immigrant and woman, and I appreciate the emotional labor you've done to help us all understand these complex issues better. Your critique of single-axis frameworks, such as noting 'you can't fix one piece and call it done,' shows a deep understanding of the concept. I'm curious, how do you think the framework of intersectionality could be applied or adapted to address the unique challenges faced by South Asian immigrant women, like your mother?

Amara, your phrase 'a woman's burdens have many handles' resonated deeply, and I appreciate how you wove your grandmother's experience as a Black, Muslim, immigrant woman in France and Guinea into our discussion on intersectionality. Your reflection shows a nuanced understanding of how intersectionality can both illuminate and sometimes struggle to capture the complexities of individual experiences. I'm grateful for the emotional labor you've done in sharing your personal connections to these concepts. For next week, I'm curious: how do you think Crenshaw's framework could be expanded or adapted to better account for experiences like your grandmother's, which don't fit neatly into American racial hierarchies?

Sofia, your analysis of how intersectionality plays out in healthcare settings is really compelling, especially when you note that 'The discrimination against Valentina isn't just unfair to her — it has actual downstream consequences on the people she's trying to help.' This shows a deep understanding of how systemic issues can have far-reaching effects. I appreciate how you've woven in your cousin's experiences to illustrate these points, which takes a lot of courage and adds richness to our class discussions. For next week, I'm curious: how do you think we can apply the concept of intersectionality to create more equitable systems, particularly in fields like healthcare where these consequences are so pronounced?

Rashida, your passion and commitment to understanding intersectionality in practice shine through in your discussion post, particularly when you say, 'The system literally could not see them. That's not an accident or an oversight, that's a structural choice about who counts.' This statement not only highlights your grasp of systemic racism but also your desire to challenge and change these systems. I appreciate the emotional labor you've done in sharing your personal experiences with discrimination in your own school, and I acknowledge the importance of your voice in this conversation. As you continue to explore these complex issues, I'm curious: how do you think we can apply Crenshaw's argument and the lessons from the DeGraffenreid case to create more inclusive and equitable systems?

Jasmine, your reflection on intersectionality, particularly your comment about how the law can't see the full picture of discrimination because it doesn't account for multiple forms of disadvantage, really stood out to me. It takes a lot of courage to share personal connections, like your family experiences, and I appreciate the emotional labor you're doing to help us all understand these complex issues better. Your question about whether intersectionality is mostly useful for analysis or if it also provides a roadmap for action is a crucial one. I'm curious to see how you continue to explore this question, especially considering how Crenshaw's work informs your thinking.

Kevin, I appreciated how you broke down the concept of intersectionality, particularly when you said, 'Being Black and a man and working class aren't three different things you experience one at a time — they interact and create a specific experience that's different from someone who shares only one of those traits.' This insight highlights the complexity of individual experiences and the limitations of single-axis frameworks. Your analytical approach to understanding intersectionality is a strength, and I'm curious to see how you'll apply this thinking to real-world scenarios. For instance, how might intersectionality inform policies or practices in a specific context, such as education or employment?

Tyler Nguyen, your discussion post stood out to me, particularly when you said, 'Rather, it is the intersection of factors such as race, class, and sexuality that determines one's positionality.' This statement highlights your nuanced understanding of how multiple axes of identity intersect and impact an individual's experiences. I appreciate how you've applied the concept of intersectionality to demonstrate the complexity of power dynamics. For further exploration, I'm curious: how do you think recognizing and addressing these intersecting factors can lead to more effective solidarity and support among marginalized groups?

Jaylen, your analysis of intersectionality as a framework for understanding how social inequalities intersect is really strong, particularly when you note that 'intersectionality recognizes that these forms of oppression are not mutually exclusive, but rather interconnected.' This shows a deep understanding of Kimberlé Crenshaw's work and its implications for social justice. I appreciate how you've applied this concept to shed light on the ways dominant groups maintain power and privilege. For further exploration, I'm curious: how do you think intersectionality could be used to address the complexities of oppression in a real-world context, such as policy-making or community organizing?

Talia, I appreciate the vulnerability and insight you shared in your reflection, particularly when you said, 'I knew that people have multiple identities and can face different kinds of discrimination, but I hadn't thought about how those forms of discrimination can actually interact to create something new instead of just stacking up.' This shows a deep understanding of intersectionality and its complexities. Your personal connection to being Latina in honors classes and experiencing discrimination firsthand adds a valuable layer of depth to our class discussions. I'm grateful for the emotional labor you've done in sharing your experiences, and I'm curious: how do you think we can apply this understanding of intersectionality to create more inclusive environments in our own communities?

Elijah, your observation that 'Systems get built with a certain kind of person in mind and everyone else has to navigate around that' really stood out to me. It shows a deep understanding of how intersectionality plays out in real-world issues, and I appreciate how you connected this idea to your own observations of policing in your neighborhood. Your use of Kimberlé Crenshaw's work to frame these issues is also a strength. As you continue to think about these issues, I'm curious: how do you think early education on social justice frameworks could help challenge and change these systemic inequalities?

Sierra, your reflection on intersectionality, particularly the way you wove together your friend Deja's experiences as a Black and deaf individual with Crenshaw's work, really stood out to me. I appreciated how you questioned the role of intersectionality, asking if it's a framework for analysis or also for action - this is a crucial distinction. Your emotional labor in sharing Deja's story and your own thoughts on the limitations of single-axis frameworks is acknowledged and valued. For next week, I'm curious: how do you think we can apply intersectionality in a way that prioritizes action and creates more inclusive spaces, like the ones you described that are welcoming to both deaf and Black individuals?

Brittany, I appreciated how you highlighted the importance of appreciating the unique qualities of each cultural group, as you said, 'I think all cultures have something beautiful about them.' Your passion for celebrating diversity is evident throughout your reflection. I also valued the personal connection you made with your friend Rosa, recognizing how her layered identity as a Latina and queer individual has contributed to her strength. For further exploration, I'm curious: how do you think we can balance appreciating cultural differences with avoiding generalizations about entire groups?

Brianna, I appreciate the vulnerability and reflection you brought to this discussion on intersectionality. Your statement, 'I'm a Black girl and I've definitely felt like sometimes I'm too much of one thing or not enough of another,' resonated deeply and highlights the importance of these conversations. It takes courage to share personal experiences of marginalization, and I acknowledge the emotional labor that goes into doing so. I'm curious, how do you think discussions like these can be further integrated into our class to create a more inclusive and supportive environment? Your insights are invaluable to this process.

Tyler, your analysis of how intersectionality plays out in real life, particularly your point that 'A Black woman experiences discrimination differently from what Black men experience and also differently from what white women experience, because she has both of those identities at once,' really stood out to me. This shows a deep understanding of Crenshaw's work and its importance for thinking about equity. I appreciate how you've applied the concept of intersectionality to highlight the unique experiences of individuals with multiple identities. For further exploration, I'm curious: how do you think we can apply this understanding of intersectionality to create more equitable environments in our own communities?

Connor, I appreciate the reflective tone in your discussion post, particularly when you said, 'I dont really see the point of focusing so much on categories and labels because I feel like that just divides people more.' This shows you're thinking deeply about the implications of intersectionality. A strength of your post is your willingness to share your honest thoughts and questions about the concept. For further exploration, I'm curious: how do you think the experiences of individuals from different backgrounds might be impacted by the approach you described, and are there any potential benefits or drawbacks to considering these categories and labels in our interactions with others?

Paige, I appreciated how you broke down the concept of intersectionality, noting that 'different aspects of identity like race, gender, class, and sexuality overlap and can lead to worse outcomes for people who have multiple marginalized identities.' This clarity shows you're really grasping the complexity of the issue. Your analysis of Kimberlé Crenshaw's work, especially the examples of how Black women are affected by the gaps in anti-discrimination frameworks, demonstrates a strong understanding of the topic. For further exploration, consider how intersectionality might play out in different contexts or industries - how might it impact policy-making or community organizing?

Jake, I appreciate how you brought your personal experience into the discussion on intersectionality, particularly when you shared that your family is white and poor, and questioned where your dad's privilege fits into the framework. This kind of critical thinking is invaluable. I'm grateful for the emotional labor you did in sharing your family's story and challenging the reading - it takes courage to do so. Your analysis of how class and economic oppression intersect with other forms of oppression is a strength of your work. For next week, I'm curious: how do you think we can apply intersectionality in a way that accounts for the complexities of economic privilege and oppression?

Cameron, I appreciated your thoughtful comment that 'Identity is layered and you can't understand someone's experience by looking at just one layer.' This insight really gets at the heart of Kimberlé Crenshaw's theory on intersectionality. It's great that you were able to distill the main point from the reading, even when you found it dense. For further exploration, I'm curious: how do you think recognizing these layered identities can change the way we approach social issues or personal relationships?

Destiny, your words really stood out to me this week, especially when you said, 'How can we sit here and read about redlining and act like it's ancient history when my neighborhood still looks exactly like the map from 1940??' This isn't just an academic concept for you, and I appreciate the emotional labor you're doing by sharing your personal experiences with racial and economic injustice. Your urgency and passion are a strength in this discussion, and I'm grateful you're bringing your whole self to this conversation. As we move forward, I'm curious: how do you think we can balance the need for calm, respectful discussion with the urgency and emotion that comes with talking about issues that affect our lives so directly?

Yolanda, I appreciate the vulnerability and reflection you brought to this discussion, especially in sharing your family member's experiences with immigration and language barriers. Your quote, 'It's not just that she's old, or just that she speaks Spanish first, or just that she cleaned houses her whole life. It's all of those things at the same time, and they all weigh different depending on where she's standing,' beautifully illustrates the complexity of intersectionality. I acknowledge the emotional labor that goes into sharing personal stories of marginalization, and I thank you for trusting us with your thoughts. As you continue to explore intersectionality, consider how these personal experiences might inform your understanding of systemic injustices. How do you think recognizing and addressing these intersecting factors could lead to more inclusive practices in various settings?

Ingrid, your words about your mom's experience as an undocumented immigrant, particularly the phrase 'he didn't exist. Not invisible exactly — people could see her. But they could act like her wellbeing didn't count,' resonated deeply. It takes a lot of courage to share personal stories, especially ones that involve vulnerability and marginalization, and I want to acknowledge the emotional labor that goes into sharing your family's history. Your ability to illustrate the intersectionality of oppressions through your mom's story is a strength of your reflection. As you continue to explore these themes, I'm curious: how do you think we can work to create spaces where people like your mom are seen and valued, not just visible?

Aiden, I appreciated your thoughtful comment about the importance of respectful dialogue when discussing intersectionality and systemic issues. You said, 'I think we can talk about intersectionality and systemic issues in a way that's respectful to everyone in the room,' which shows you're considering the impact of our conversations on others. One strength of your reflection is how you're applying the concept of intersectionality to real-world conversations. As you continue to think about these issues, I'm curious: how might you balance the need for respectful dialogue with the importance of amplifying marginalized voices and perspectives?

DeShawn, your passionate sharing of your family's experiences, particularly the comparison between how your brother and his white classmates are treated in school, is a powerful illustration of intersectionality in practice. I appreciate how you explicitly stated that you learned about these issues from watching your family, and now you're recognizing them in the context of our readings. Your willingness to share your personal story and connect it to broader social issues is a strength of your reflection. As you continue to explore these topics, I'm curious: how do you think educators and policymakers can work to address the intersecting issues of racial bias, socioeconomic status, and ability that affect students like your brother?

Camille, I appreciated how you highlighted the limitations of the BMI, noting that it was developed using only white European men as the data set. This critique of dominant narratives is a great example of intersectionality in action, as you also pointed out in your discussion of how a person's experience at the doctor can't be reduced to just one aspect of their identity. One strength of your analysis is how you applied the concept of intersectionality to a real-world issue, making it more relatable and understandable. For further exploration, I'm curious: how do you think healthcare providers can work to dismantle these biases and create a more inclusive approach to health evaluations?

Imani, your reflection on how being Black and a girl creates a unique experience that can't be reduced to just the sum of its parts really stood out to me. I appreciate how you shared your personal experience and connected it to Crenshaw's work on intersectionality, showing a deep understanding of the concept. Your comment about the 'texture' of how teachers speak to you versus your friends was particularly insightful. I'm grateful for the emotional labor you've done in sharing your lived reality with us. As you continue to explore intersectionality, I'm curious: how do you think we can apply this framework to create more equitable spaces in our own communities?

Jordan, your words really stood out to me this week, especially when you said, 'the intersections are real even when the essay isn't perfect.' It takes a lot of courage to share your personal experiences with dyslexia, ADHD, and being Latino, and I want to acknowledge the emotional labor that goes into that. Your application of intersectionality to your own life, as inspired by Crenshaw's work, shows a deep understanding of the concept. I'm curious, how do you think we can work to create spaces where all aspects of our identities are valued and seen, rather than simplified or overlooked?
